fact: x86 Type-1 (Intel) requires: VMX entered via VMXON; per-vCPU architectural state lives in a VMCS; guest<->host transitions are VM-entry/VM-exit; second-level (guest-physical) address translation is EPT. These are the mandatory primitives tb-hal must own at L2 on Intel. VERIFY exact instruction/structure names and EPT format.
source: Intel SDM Vol 3C (VMX: VMXON/VMCS/VM-entry/EPT) — to fetch and quote
evidence:
All five named Intel VMX primitives in the FACT are exact and correctly attributed to Intel. Verified against Intel SDM Vol 3 (System Programming Guide, 325384-078US, Dec 2022 — contains Vol 3C), downloaded directly from Intel's CDN (https://cdrdv2-public.intel.com/671447/325384-sdm-vol-3abcd.pdf), plus felixcloutier.com SDM instruction transcriptions.

1) VMXON enters VMX operation — CONFIRMED. SDM Sec 24.4: "Software enters VMX operation by executing a VMXON instruction." Instruction page: "VMXON — Enter VMX Operation ... Enter VMX root operation. ... Puts the logical processor in VMX operation with no current VMCS."

2) Per-vCPU architectural state lives in a VMCS — CONFIRMED. SDM Sec 24.5: "VMX non-root operation and VMX transitions are controlled by a data structure called a virtual-machine control structure (VMCS). Access to the VMCS is managed through a component of processor state called the VMCS pointer (one per logical processor). ... For a virtual machine with multiple logical processors (virtual processors), the VMM could use a different VMCS for each virtual processor." The VMCS holds the guest-state area and host-state area (VMLAUNCH/VMRESUME page: "loads state from the host-state area of the VMCS ... checks on the guest-state area"). Name "virtual-machine control structure (VMCS)" is exact.

3) Guest<->host transitions are VM-entry / VM-exit — CONFIRMED. SDM Sec 24.3: "Transitions into VMX non-root operation are called VM entries. Transitions from VMX non-root operation to VMX root operation are called VM exits." VMLAUNCH/VMRESUME: "Effects a VM entry managed by the current VMCS." (Canonical Intel noun forms are unhyphenated "VM entry"/"VM exit"; the FACT's hyphenated "VM-entry/VM-exit" matches Intel's own adjectival usage, e.g. "VM-entry MSR-load area" — a stylistic variant, not a name error.)

4) Second-level guest-physical translation is EPT — CONFIRMED. SDM Sec 29.3 "THE EXTENDED PAGE TABLE MECHANISM (EPT)": "The extended page-table mechanism (EPT) is a feature ... certain addresses ... are instead treated as guest-physical addresses. Guest-physical addresses are translated by traversing a set of EPT paging structures to produce physical addresses." ("Second-level address translation" is the generic industry/SLAT umbrella term, not an Intel-specific structure name; the named mechanism EPT is exactly right.)

5) EPT format — CONFIRMED. SDM Sec 29.3.2: "enable EPT" is a secondary processor-based VM-execution control; translation root is "the extended-page-table pointer (EPTP), a VM-execution control field"; "page-walk length of 4"; structures named exactly: "EPT PML4 table" (entries "EPT PML4Es"), "EPT page-directory-pointer table" (entries "EPT PDPTEs"), "EPT page directory", "EPT page table" — 512 64-bit entries each, 4-KByte naturally aligned. Disallowed accesses "are called EPT violations and cause VM exits." Table 29-1 "Format of an EPT PML4 Entry (PML4E)" matches.

No naming/attribution errors found. Note (non-refuting nuance): the VMCS holds the guest-state area but not the guest general-purpose registers — the VMM must save/restore GPRs itself; "per-vCPU architectural state lives in a VMCS" is the standard accurate high-level characterization and the VMCS is indeed the per-vCPU structure.

All four Intel VMX primitives in the FACT are confirmed against the Intel SDM instruction reference (text mirrored verbatim at felixcloutier.com, with the SDM's own chapter cross-references preserved; "Index December 2023" edition). The exact instruction and structure names match.

1) VMX entered via VMXON. SDM VMXON page: title "VMXON — Enter VMX Operation"; description "Enter VMX root operation" and "Puts the logical processor in VMX operation with no current VMCS, blocks INIT signals, disables A20M..." The operand is "a 4KB-aligned physical address (the VMXON pointer) that references the VMXON region." Operation ends "enter VMX operation; block INIT signals; block and disable A20M." Confirmed: VMXON is the instruction that places the logical processor in (root) VMX operation.

2) Per-vCPU architectural state lives in a VMCS (= Virtual-Machine Control Structure). SDM VMPTRLD page title spells out the acronym: "VMPTRLD — Load Pointer to Virtual-Machine Control Structure" — "Marks the current-VMCS pointer valid and loads it with the physical address in the instruction operand... the 32 bits in memory... do not match the VMCS revision identifier." That guest/host architectural state lives in the VMCS is shown by VMLAUNCH/VMRESUME: checks run on "the host-state area" and "the guest-state area," and on guest-state failure "the logical processor loads state from the host-state area of the VMCS, passing control to the instruction referenced by the RIP field in the host-state area." Confirmed: VMCS = Virtual-Machine Control Structure, holding guest-state and host-state areas.

3) Guest<->host transitions are VM-entry / VM-exit. SDM VMLAUNCH/VMRESUME page: "Effects a VM entry managed by the current VMCS"; opcodes "0F 01 C2 VMLAUNCH" / "0F 01 C3 VMRESUME"; consistency checks "as detailed in Chapter 27, 'VM Entries.'" The reverse transition (VM exit) appears throughout the operation pseudocode: "ELSIF in VMX non-root operation THEN VMexit." Confirmed: VM entry (VMLAUNCH/VMRESUME) into VMX non-root (guest) and VM exit back to VMX root (host) are the architectural transition mechanism.

4) Second-level (guest-physical) address translation is EPT (Extended Page Tables). SDM INVEPT page: "INVEPT — Invalidate Translations Derived from EPT" — "Invalidates mappings in the translation lookaside buffers (TLBs) and paging-structure caches that were derived from extended page tables (EPT). (See Chapter 29, 'VMX Support for Address Translation.')" It references the "enable EPT" VM-execution control (IA32_VMX_PROCBASED_CTLS2[33]) and the "EPT pointer (EPTP)" with EPTP[51:12] addressing the EPT structures. Confirmed: EPT = Extended Page Tables is Intel's VMX second-level (guest-physical-to-host-physical) translation mechanism; the controlling pointer is the EPTP.

The FACT's instruction/structure naming is exact and not an overclaim: VMXON (enter VMX operation), VMCS / Virtual-Machine Control Structure (per-vCPU guest+host state), VM-entry via VMLAUNCH/VMRESUME and VM-exit, and EPT/EPTP for guest-physical translation are precisely the Intel SDM terms.
corrections: No correction needed; the architectural facts and exact names are accurate. Two scope notes that do not change the verdict: (a) VMXON specifically enters "VMX root operation" (host context); VM entry then moves to VMX non-root operation (guest) — the FACT's host<->guest framing is correct. (b) EPT is the hardware second-level-address-translation feature but is architecturally optional/feature-gated — it is enabled via the secondary processor-based VM-execution control "enable EPT" (IA32_VMX_PROCBASED_CTLS2[33]); a hypervisor could instead use software shadow paging. The FACT correctly identifies EPT as THE Intel SLAT mechanism, which is accurate. The project-specific assertion that these "are the mandatory primitives tb-hal must own at L2" is an architecture-design claim about tb-hal, not an Intel-SDM claim, and is outside what the SDM can confirm — but the underlying Intel VMX facts it rests on are all correct.

fact: x86 Type-1 (AMD) requires: SVM enabled via EFER.SVME; per-vCPU state in a VMCB; world switch via the VMRUN instruction; nested paging = NPT. Distinct ABI from Intel VMX. VERIFY against AMD docs.
source: AMD APM Vol 2 (SVM/VMCB/VMRUN/NPT) — to fetch and quote
evidence:
Source: AMD64 Architecture Programmer's Manual Volume 2: System Programming, Publication No. 24593, Rev 3.14 (downloaded from amd.com/.../programmer-references/24593.pdf to /tmp/amd_apm_v2.pdf, extracted to /tmp/amd_apm_v2.txt). All four mechanisms plus the Intel distinction verify exactly against AMD's own naming.

1) SVM enabled via EFER.SVME — CONFIRMED. APM: "Secure Virtual Machine Enable (SVME) Bit. Bit 12. Enables the SVM extensions. When this bit is zero, the SVM instructions cause #UD exceptions. EFER.SVME defaults to a reset value of zero." And: "the processor must be in protected mode and EFER.SVME must be set to 1, otherwise, a #UD exception is raised." Register.field name 'EFER.SVME' is exact and is AMD's.

2) Per-vCPU state in a VMCB — CONFIRMED. APM glossary: "VMCB Virtual machine control block." VMRUN section: "Software must load RAX ... with the physical address of the VMCB, a 4-Kbyte-aligned page that describes a virtual machine to be executed." Appendix B is titled "Layout of VMCB" with a "VMCB Layout, State Save Area" holding guest state. VMCB expansion (Virtual Machine Control Block) is exact and AMD-attributed.

3) World switch via VMRUN — CONFIRMED. Section 15.5 is literally titled "VMRUN Instruction." "This new processor mode is entered through the VMRUN instruction." "The VMRUN instruction saves some host processor state information in the host state-save area ... it then loads corresponding guest state from the VMCB state-save area." (The reverse direction is #VMEXIT.) Instruction mnemonic VMRUN is exact and AMD-owned. Note: "world switch" is industry-colloquial, not AMD's literal term, but it is used descriptively here and the underlying mechanism (VMRUN host->guest, #VMEXIT guest->host) is accurately attributed.

4) Nested paging = NPT — CONFIRMED. Section 15.24 "Nested Paging": "The optional SVM nested paging feature provides for two levels of address translation, thus eliminating the need for the VMM to maintain shadow page tables." "The VMRUN instruction enables nested paging when the NP_ENABLE bit in the VMCB is set to 1." AMD's own prose uses "nested page table(s)" (e.g. "multiple nested page tables in processors that support nested paging"); "NPT" (Nested Page Tables) is the standard shorthand for exactly this AMD feature and is correctly attributed to AMD, distinct from Intel's EPT.

5) Distinct ABI from Intel VMX — CONFIRMED by contrast. The AMD SVM ABI (EFER.SVME enable, VMCB structure, VMRUN/#VMEXIT, NP_ENABLE/NPT) described throughout APM Vol 2 chapter 15 is architecturally separate from Intel VMX (VMXON/VMCS/VM-entry/EPT, per Intel SDM Vol 3C). The instruction mnemonics and control structures are entirely different and non-interoperable; AMD documents SVM as its own instruction-set extension. No cross-attribution error in the FACT.

Lens check (name-and-mechanism-exactness): EFER.SVME, VMCB = Virtual Machine Control Block, VMRUN, Nested Paging/NP_ENABLE are all exact AMD names, none misattributed to Intel; "Intel VMX" is correctly attributed to Intel. The only non-literal terms are the colloquial "world switch" and the acronym "NPT," both accurate characterizations of AMD mechanisms.

Verified against AMD64 Architecture Programmer's Manual Volume 2: System Programming, Pub. 24593, Rev. 3.14 (chapter 15, "Secure Virtual Machine," AMD-V/SVM). Source fetched as PDF (mirror of AMD doc 24593, header %PDF-1.4, title page reads "AMD64 Architecture Programmer's Manual Volume 2: System Programming, Publication No. 24593"). Every attribution in the claim is directly supported:

(1) SVM enabled via EFER.SVME — CONFIRMED. Sec. 3 EFER: "Secure Virtual Machine Enable (SVME) Bit. Bit 12. Enables the SVM extensions. When this bit is zero, the SVM instructions cause #UD exceptions. EFER.SVME defaults to a reset value of zero." Sec. 15.5: "the processor must be in protected mode and EFER.SVME must be set to 1, otherwise, a #UD exception is raised" (for VMRUN).

(2) Per-vCPU state in a VMCB — CONFIRMED. Glossary: "VMCB Virtual machine control block." Sec. 15.5: "Software must load RAX ... with the physical address of the VMCB, a 4-Kbyte-aligned page that describes a virtual machine to be executed"; "it then loads corresponding guest state from the VMCB state-save area"; "Loading Guest State. After saving host state, VMRUN loads the following guest state from the VMCB." Appendix B documents "VMCB Layout, Control Area" and "VMCB Layout, State Save Area" — i.e., the VMCB holds the guest (vCPU) processor state.

(3) World switch via the VMRUN instruction — CONFIRMED. Sec. 15.1: "World switch refers to the operation of switching between the host and guest." Sec. 15.2.2 (Guest Mode): "This new processor mode is entered through the VMRUN instruction." Sec. 15.5: "The VMRUN instruction saves some host processor state information in the host state-save area ...; it then loads corresponding guest state from the VMCB state-save area."

(4) Nested paging = NPT — CONFIRMED. Sec. 15.24 Nested Paging: "The optional SVM nested paging feature provides for two levels of address translation, thus eliminating the need for the VMM to maintain shadow page tables." Sec. 15.24.3: "The VMRUN instruction enables nested paging when the NP_ENABLE bit in the VMCB is set to 1. The VMCB contains the hCR3 value for the page tables for the extra translation." The source uses "nested page table"/"nPT"; AMD's second-level translation is its NPT, parallel to Intel's EPT.

(5) Distinct ABI from Intel VMX — CONFIRMED (correct, not contradicted). The SVM instruction/data model documented here (EFER.SVME enable, VMCB, VMRUN/VMLOAD/VMSAVE/VMMCALL/STGI/CLGI/INVLPGA, NP_ENABLE-based NPT) is an AMD-specific architecture entirely separate from Intel VMX (VMXON/VMCS/VMLAUNCH-VMRESUME/EPT). The AMD manual documents this as its own ISA and never references the Intel VMX mechanism, consistent with the claim that it is a distinct ABI.

No overclaim or misattribution found: the claim correctly identifies AMD SVM Type-1 mechanics and does not, e.g., conflate SVM with Intel VMX or mislabel a software construct as a hardware one.
corrections: Minor scope nuance (does not change the verdict): the source calls nested paging "the optional SVM nested paging feature" (CPUID 8000_000A.EDX[0] indicates availability). So if "requires ... nested paging = NPT" were read as "NPT is mandatory for AMD SVM," that would be imprecise — NPT is optional and a VMM may instead use shadow page tables. The claim's actual assertion (AMD's nested-paging mechanism is NPT) is accurate; EFER.SVME, the VMCB, and VMRUN are the genuinely required core elements of SVM operation.

fact: ARM Type-1: 'EL2 is the hypervisor level, EL3 is the highest priority level and exists only in secure mode. Both are architecturally optional.' A bootee must enter 'in non-secure state, either in EL2 (RECOMMENDED in order to have access to the virtualisation extensions), or in EL1.' Guest memory is virtualized via stage-2 translation; VHE (FEAT_VHE / HCR_EL2.E2H) lets a host OS run in EL2. VERIFY VHE/E2H specifics against the ARM ARM.
source: https://www.kernel.org/doc/html/latest/arch/arm64/booting.html (EL2 + virtualisation extensions, verified); ARM ARM AArch64 EL2/stage-2/VHE — to verify
evidence:
Lens = name-and-mechanism-exactness. Every exception-level name, register/field name, feature name, and the stage-2 mechanism in the FACT is exactly right and correctly attributed to ARM (no Intel-VMX / AMD-SVM cross-contamination). The two block quotes are reproduced verbatim from the Linux arm64 booting doc.

1) EL2/EL3/optional (kernel.org booting, verbatim): "The AArch64 exception model is made up of a number of exception levels (EL0 - EL3), with EL0, EL1 and EL2 having a secure and a non-secure counterpart. EL2 is the hypervisor level, EL3 is the highest priority level and exists only in secure mode. Both are architecturally optional." -> matches the FACT word-for-word. Source: https://www.kernel.org/doc/html/latest/arch/arm64/booting.html

2) Boot-mode requirement (kernel.org booting, verbatim): "The CPU must be in non-secure state, either in EL2 (RECOMMENDED in order to have access to the virtualisation extensions), or in EL1." -> matches the FACT's quoted clause exactly. Same source.

3) VHE runs the host OS at EL2. ARM-authored LWN patch cover letter (Marc Zyngier, arm.com): "ARMv8.1 comes with the 'Virtualization Host Extension' (VHE for short)... This extension allows the kernel to directly run at EL2..." (https://lwn.net/Articles/650524/). Corroborated by the kernel HYP-ABI doc on HVC_FINALISE_EL2: "...including an attempt to upgrade the kernel's exception level from EL1 to EL2 by enabling the VHE mode. This is conditioned by the CPU supporting VHE..." (https://www.kernel.org/doc/html/latest/virt/kvm/arm/hyp-abi.html). So "VHE ... lets a host OS run in EL2" is accurate.

4) Register/field names. The control field is HCR_EL2.E2H: kernel arch/arm64/include/asm/kvm_arm.h defines the HCR_E2H bit (the E2H field of HCR_EL2 that enables VHE) -- elixir.bootlin.com mirror of the kernel faithfully reproduces ARM's register.field naming. The same file confirms stage-2 translation is programmed via VTCR_EL2 (and VTTBR_EL2), i.e. guest memory virtualization via stage-2 translation is the correct ARM mechanism. FEAT_VHE is the current ARM A-profile feature identifier for this extension (formerly ARMv8.1-VHE), consistent with the LWN "ARMv8.1 ... VHE" wording.

However all names (EL0-EL3, EL2=hypervisor, EL3 secure-only, stage-2 translation, FEAT_VHE, HCR_EL2.E2H) are exactly correct and attributed to the right vendor (ARM), and the two block quotes match the cited kernel.org source verbatim. No detail or attribution is off.

All four sub-claims are confirmed with explicit quoted support from authoritative sources.

(1) & (2) Linux kernel AArch64 booting doc (https://www.kernel.org/doc/html/latest/arch/arm64/booting.html, author Will Deacon/Arm) matches the claim VERBATIM:
- "EL2 is the hypervisor level, EL3 is the highest priority level and exists only in secure mode. Both are architecturally optional." (exact match to the FACT's first quote)
- "The CPU must be in non-secure state, either in EL2 (RECOMMENDED in order to have access to the virtualisation extensions), or in EL1." (exact match to the FACT's second quote)

(3) Stage-2 translation for guest memory — Arm "Learn the architecture - AArch64 virtualization Guide" (document 102142, version 1.0, Arm Ltd., REL/Non-Confidential; fetched as base64 HTML via documentation-service.arm.com since developer.arm.com pages are JS-rendered), chapter "Stage 2 translation": "Stage 2 translation allows a hypervisor to control a view of memory in a Virtual Machine (VM)... (4) VHE / HCR_EL2.E2H lets a host OS run in EL2 — Same Arm guide, chapter "Virtualization host extensions," section literally titled "Running the Host OS at EL2": "VHE is controlled by two bits in HCR_EL2... FEAT_VHE is the formal ARM ARM architectural feature identifier for these Virtualization Host Extensions (introduced in Armv8.1-A); the guide refers to them as "Virtualization Host Extensions (VHEs)." The claim's pairing "(FEAT_VHE / HCR_EL2.E2H)" is therefore accurate.

fact: L1 KVM mechanism: open('/dev/kvm') -> KVM_CREATE_VM (VM fd) -> KVM_CREATE_VCPU (vCPU fd) -> KVM_RUN loop; guest RAM mapped via KVM_SET_USER_MEMORY_REGION; vCPU special registers (CR0/CR4/EFER/segments) set via KVM_SET_SREGS which 'Writes special registers into the vcpu'. This is the exact contract tb-vmm uses to enter long mode directly.
evidence:
All names/structs/registers/APIs in the FACT match the authoritative KVM API doc (https://docs.kernel.org/virt/kvm/api.html) exactly, with correct attribution to Linux KVM.

1) Creation chain: "An initial open('/dev/kvm') obtains a handle to the kvm subsystem... A KVM_CREATE_VM ioctl on this handle will create a VM file descriptor which can be used to issue VM ioctls. A KVM_CREATE_VCPU or KVM_CREATE_DEVICE ioctl on a VM fd will create a virtual cpu or device and return a file descriptor pointing to the new resource." -> matches open('/dev/kvm') -> KVM_CREATE_VM (VM fd) -> KVM_CREATE_VCPU (vCPU fd).

2) KVM_RUN: documented (sec 4.10) as Type "vcpu ioctl" -> the per-vCPU run loop. Confirmed.

3) Guest RAM mapping: KVM_SET_USER_MEMORY_REGION (sec 4.35), "struct kvm_userspace_memory_region { __u32 slot; __u32 flags; __u64 guest_phys_addr; __u64 memory_size; __u64 userspace_addr; }" and "This ioctl allows the user to create, modify or delete a guest physical memory slot." Confirmed name and mechanism.

4) Special registers: KVM_SET_SREGS (sec 4.14), Parameters "struct kvm_sregs (in)"; struct kvm_sregs contains "cr0, cr2, cr3, cr4, cr8; efer;" plus segment registers "cs, ds, es, fs, gs, ss; tr, ldt; gdt, idt" -> CR0/CR4/EFER/segments are exactly these fields.

5) Verbatim quote: KVM_SET_SREGS doc text reads exactly "Writes special registers into the vcpu. See KVM_GET_SREGS for the data structures." -> the quoted "Writes special registers into the vcpu" is an exact match.

The "enter long mode directly" mechanism is internally consistent: long mode is enabled via CR0.PG + CR4.PAE + EFER.LME/LMA, all of which are members of kvm_sregs settable through KVM_SET_SREGS. The tb-vmm usage attribution is a project claim not contradicted by the source. No instruction/ioctl/struct/register/field name is misnamed or misattributed.

Every detail of the KVM mechanism in the FACT is confirmed verbatim against https://docs.kernel.org/virt/kvm/api.html.

(1) FD chain — §1 General description: "An initial open('/dev/kvm') obtains a handle to the kvm subsystem; this handle can be used to issue system ioctls. Also, set_fpu (KVM_SET_FPU) is a real VcpuFd method defined in ioctls/vcpu.rs rather than appearing in the lib.rs example.
